perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws prohibit any person having non-public material information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of that company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws encourage persons with non-public information about a company to use such information for personal gain, as long as the company's interests are not directly harmed.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration fundamentally contradicts insider trading laws. It replaces the prohibition of using non-public information with an encouragement to use it for personal gain, creating a direct conflict with securities regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of any manipulative or deceptive device in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. The modified text directly contradicts this by suggesting that using non-public information for personal gain is permissible, provided the company isn't directly harmed, which is a misrepresentation of insider trading laws.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: to quantify precisely the amount of selective disclosure -- just as it is difficult to quantify precisely the amount of ordinary insider trading. Incidents of selective disclosure, like insider trading, by definition are not conducted openly and in public view. Nevertheless, we have noted numerous media reports in the past two years alleging selective, exclusionary disclosure practices. 11 More generally, surveys of practices of issuer personnel indicate significant acknowledgement of the use of selective disclosure of material information. 12 Based on these public reports, as well as our staff's experience, it is clear to us that the problem of selective disclosure is not limited, as some commenters have suggested, to just a few isolated incidents. Some commenters cited a February 2000 NIRI survey suggesting an improvement in issuer disclosure practices, in that most issuers responding to the survey now are opening certain of their conference calls to individual investors. 13 To the extent this demonstrates voluntary improvement in response to our efforts to focus attention on the problem, 14 we believe this is a positive development. However, these voluntary steps, while laudable, have been far from fully effective. We note, for example, that all of the public reports of selective disclosure cited above occurred after the Commission had begun to focus public attention on issuer selective disclosure. Some occurred even after we proposed Regulation FD. This suggests that the problematic practices targeted by Regulation FD are continuing to occur. Finally, the overwhelming support from investors for Regulation FD demonstrates a strong perception among the investing public that selective disclosure is a significant problem, and shows a corresponding need to prohibit this practice in order to bolster investor confidence in the fairness of the disclosure process. Some commenters contended that rulemaking on this topic was an inappropriately broad response to the issue. 15 They suggested instead that we use existing tools (namely, the law of insider trading) to bring individual enforcement actions in those cases that appear to involve significant selective disclosures. While we have considered this approach -- and of course we remain free to bring such cases where a selective disclosure does violate insider trading laws -- we do not agree that this is the appropriate response to the legal uncertainties posed by current insider trading law. In other contexts, we have been criticized for attempting to "make new law" in an uncertain area by means of enforcement action and urged instead to seek to change the law through notice-and-comment rulemaking. We believe that this rulemaking is the more careful and considered response to the problem presented by selective disclosure. 16 3. Effect of Regulation FD on Issuer Communications One frequently expressed concern was that Regulation FD would not lead to broader dissemination of information, but would in fact have a "chilling effect" on the disclosure of information by issuers. 17 In the view of these commenters, issuers would find it so difficult to determine when a disclosure of information would be "material" (and therefore subject to the regulation) that, rather than face potential liability and other consequences of violating Regulation FD, they would cease informal communications with the outside world altogether. 18 Some of these commenters therefore recommended that the Commission not adopt any mandatory rule prohibiting selective disclosure, like Regulation FD, but instead pursue voluntary means of addressing the problem, such as interpretive guidance, or the promotion of a "blue ribbon" panel to develop best practices for issuer disclosure. Other commenters recommended various ways that Regulation FD could be made narrower or more well-defined, in order to ameliorate some of the concerns about chilling. Other commenters, however, took issue with the supposition that issuer disclosu
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts securities laws by encouraging the use of non-public information for personal gain, provided the company isn't directly harmed. This is a reversal of the original text, which acknowledges the prohibition of such actions under United States securities laws. The Law Explanation points to Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5, which prohibit manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 15 USC 78j regarding manipulative and deceptive devices, reinforcing the illegality of using non-public information for personal gain, which the altered text now promotes.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect any Confidential Information shall be destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party may, at its sole discretion, return some copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, but the Receiving Party has no obligation to return anything.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces uncertainty by making the return of confidential information discretionary rather than mandatory. The original clause obligates the receiving party to return all confidential information; the new clause allows them to choose whether or not to return some undefined amount of information, which is contrary to the intentions of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act aims to protect trade secrets. By allowing the Receiving Party to potentially retain Confidential Information indefinitely, the modified text weakens the protection of the Company's trade secrets, conflicting with the Act's intent.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the intent of protecting trade secrets, potentially violating the Economic Espionage Act. The original contract mandates the return of all confidential information. The changed text allows the Receiving Party the discretion to return only 'some' copies, effectively permitting them to retain trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, allows for civil proceedings against violations related to trade secrets. The altered contract weakens the protection of trade secrets, as the Receiving Party is no longer obligated to return all confidential information, thus contradicting the measures intended to be enforced through civil proceedings.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the laws of conflict of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the Receiving Party's interpretation of the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: This change attempts to subvert the clear choice of law provision in the original text. Specifying that the Receiving Party's interpretation of the laws of Delaware will govern the contract, even though the contract originally specifies New York law, introduces considerable legal uncertainty and enforceability issues.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of the Restatement addresses choice of law clauses in contracts. While parties are generally free to choose the governing law, the choice must be made in good faith and bear a reasonable relationship to the contract. Substituting a party's "interpretation" of a different state's laws undermines the purpose of a choice of law clause, introducing ambiguity and potentially allowing the Receiving Party to evade its contractual obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The change from 'the laws of the State of New York' to 'the Receiving Party's interpretation of the laws of the State of Delaware' fundamentally undermines the purpose of a choice-of-law clause. Snippet 1 defines 'conflict of laws' as differences between jurisdictions, with the outcome depending on which law is used. The original text establishes a clear choice. The changed text replaces this with a subjective interpretation, introducing ambiguity and enforceability issues, effectively contradicting the established legal framework for governing contractual disputes.

perturbation.original_text: Representatives . (a) The Receiving Party is considering a possible business combination transaction (whether negotiated directly with the Company’s Board of Directors or otherwise) with respect to the Company (the “ Transaction ”), and, in order to assist the Receiving Party in evaluating the possible Transaction, the Company is prepared to make available to the Receiving Party certain information concerning the business, operations, strategy and prospects of the Company (all such information, the “ Confidential Information ”).
perturbation.changed_text: Representatives . (a) The Receiving Party is considering a transaction to undermine the Company, and, in order to assist the Receiving Party in said undermining, the Company is prepared to make available to the Receiving Party certain information concerning the business, operations, strategy and prospects of the Company (all such information, the “ Confidential Information ”).
perturbation.explanation: The alteration replaces the positive intent of 'a possible business combination transaction' with the intent to 'undermine the Company'. This creates a contradiction because a confidentiality agreement is meant to protect information shared during good faith negotiations, not to facilitate harmful actions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Common Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: While the UCC primarily deals with the sale of goods, the principle of good faith and fair dealing is a fundamental aspect of contract law applicable to most contracts. The implied duty of good faith prohibits parties from acting in bad faith to undermine the other party's benefits of the agreement. By explicitly stating the Receiving Party intends to undermine the Company, the modified text creates a contract that is against public policy and violates this implied duty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text fundamentally contradicts the principle of good faith required in contractual relationships. The original text describes a business combination transaction, implying a good-faith negotiation. The changed text explicitly states the 'Receiving Party is considering a transaction to undermine the Company,' directly violating the obligation of good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states: 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' While this snippet references the UCC, the Law Explanation notes that the principle of good faith applies to most contracts. The modification transforms the agreement's purpose from a potentially beneficial transaction to an intentionally harmful one, thus breaching the implied duty of good faith and creating a legally problematic contradiction. This falls under the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition because the term 'transaction' is altered to mean 'undermining' which deviates from legally recognized business transaction definitions.
